exemplar.title:
What is the optimal timing and dosage of paracetamol to maximize its
therapeutic effect?

What is the optimal timing and dosage of paracetamol to maximize its

subject.title: Mathematics Applications & Interpretation (AI)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics: Applications and Interpretation** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **real-world applications of mathematics, statistical analysis, mathematical modeling, and data interpretation**, with a strong focus on **technology use, practical problem-solving, and interpreting mathematical results in context**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | External examination (calculator permitted) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 2** | External examination (technology required) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | External examination (technology required) | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal assessment | 30h | 30h | 20% |

strengths: Sections are clearly sign-posted with headings, tables and graphs, facilitating navigation
Title page clearly states investigation focus and session
Figure 1 effectively visualises the paracetamol context
Guiding questions are clearly articulated and align with the investigationâs aim
weaknesses: Introduction contains digressions into packaging and availability that interrupt logical flow
Some subsections feel long-winded and repetitive (e.g. repeated aims, duplicated NHS references)
Conclusion paragraph is overly dense and could be more concise
Transitions between sections (data extraction to pharmacokinetic modelling) are sometimes abrupt
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration demonstrates adequate structure with an introduction, body and conclusion, matching band 2 overall. While organization and signposting are serviceable, occasional digressions, repetition and abrupt transitions prevent it from reaching the clarity and conciseness of higher bands. A score of 2 reflects this mix of strengths and limitations.

strengths: Consistent use of appropriate symbols (Câââ, k_el, t) and units throughout
Effective use of multiple representations (scatter plots, ln P vs t linearisation, tables, Excel charts)
Key data tables (e.g. Table 1 and Table 3) clearly organise extracted measurements and dosing outcomes
weaknesses: Notation is inconsistent (mixing âÂµg/mLâ vs âug/mLâ, variable cases and bolding)
Graphs sometimes lack axis labels and regression equation annotations
Misstatements in series formula for infinite summation and conflated definitions of constants introduce confusion
Some explanatory text is muddled and references graphics without clear linkage
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the exploration uses a variety of correct mathematical representations and notation, lapses in consistency, clarity of definitions and formula accuracy limit its communication effectiveness. These mixed strengths align with the descriptor for band 2, justifying a score of 2.

strengths: Significant independent thinking shown by extracting data points from a published graph using GeoGebra
A clear personal motivation (frequent paracetamol use) drives the choice and framing of the investigation
Creative application of geometric series modelling to cumulative dosage challenges standard textbook treatments
weaknesses: Model choices, while creative, remain within the expected SL toolkit rather than breaking new ground
Personal approach informs the investigation but does not consistently enrich the mathematical insights to an exceptional degree
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student shows clear evidence of independent thinking, personal engagement and creative initiative beyond minimal expectations, but does not rise to the âoutstandingâ level. Band 2 accurately reflects these achievements.

strengths: Meaningful reflection ties calculated concentrations back to therapeutic windows and guiding questions
The student discusses real-world dosing advice in light of modelling results
Calculation of Sââ19.5 Âµg/mL is explicitly connected to clinical relevance
weaknesses: Critical analysis remains basic, with only limited critique of mathematical assumptions and error sources
Connections to modelling limitations (instant absorption, single-compartment) are suggested but not deeply explored
Reflection could be strengthened by quantifying safety margins and probing error propagation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission contains meaningful reflective elements and a clear connection to understanding, yet lacks the depth of critical analysis required for band 3. A score of 2 reflects substantial reflection without fully critical depth.

strengths: The pharmacokinetic model P(t)=Câââ e^{âk_el(tâb)} is correctly structured and applied
Exponential regression and geometric-series sums are executed accurately at appropriate SL level
Conceptual understanding of first-order kinetics and series convergence is consistently demonstrated
weaknesses: Minor errors in y-intercept extraction and occasional unit typos appear
Typographical mistakes in logarithmic values and slope calculations (use of two extreme points rather than regression)
Sigma notation and error-margin considerations could streamline and deepen the mathematical presentation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The mathematics used is relevant to the research question, mostly correct and demonstrates good conceptual understanding of SL-level techniques. Although there are minor inaccuracies and opportunities for greater rigour, the work clearly meets the band 3 descriptor, warranting a score of 3.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student demonstrates notable initiative by extracting numerical data from a published graph using GeoGebra and by choosing to model cumulative dosage with a geometric seriesâboth go beyond a textbook approach. This shows âsignificant evidence of independent thinking,â warranting band 2 but not the âoutstandingâ originality of band 3.

The student shows good conceptual understanding of exponential decay, first-order kinetics and series convergence, explaining why linearisation is valid and interpreting r < 1. However, deeper pharmacokinetic nuances (e.g., volume of distribution) are not addressed, so understanding is âgood in partsâ/throughout rather than âthorough with rigourâ. Band 3 is therefore most fitting.

Calculations are largely correct: k_el is obtained from the ln-plot gradient, geometric-series sums are executed accurately, and units are mostly consistent. Nonetheless, minor slips (using 13.4 as y-intercept instead of ln C value, occasional unit typos) indicate âmostly correctâ rather than fully correct with only minor errors, supporting band 3.

Most material is relevant to finding an optimal dosage, but there is noticeable repetition (multiple restatements of aims, duplicated NHS references) and several long paragraphs that could be condensed. This aligns with âmostly relevant with some unnecessary elements,â placing the work in band 2 rather than the more concise expectations of band 3.

The student consistently uses symbols such as C_max, k_el, t and exponential notation, and units (Âµg mLâ»Â¹, hr) appear throughout. Minor lapses (mixing âÂµg/mLâ and âug/mLâ, inconsistent bolding) prevent full accuracy, but the language is largely appropriate, satisfying the descriptor for band 3.

Explanations are usually understandable, but inconsistency in notation (capital vs lower-case e, switching variables) and some muddled sentences (âI elucidate later in this explorationâ) reduce clarity. Overall communication is âmostly clear with some consistencyâ, corresponding to band 2.

Exponential decay, linearisation and infinite geometric series are solidly within the SL AI syllabus and directly relevant to the research question. The mathematics is appropriately chosen but not sophisticated enough (e.g., no differential-equation systems or statistical fitting) for higher bands, so band 3 is appropriate.

The exploration employs multiple representations: original scientific graph, re-plotted scatter diagrams, linearised ln P vs t plot, geometric-series tables and several Excel charts. These are integrated to support arguments (e.g., using the linear graph to justify first-order kinetics). While aesthetic quality is mixed, the variety and generally correct interpretation constitute âeffective use,â fitting band 3.

The student reflects on results by comparing maximal concentrations with therapeutic ranges and discussing how interval length affects accumulation. This constitutes meaningful reflection rather than a simple restatement, aligning with band 2. Depth is not sufficient for âsubstantial critical reflectionâ.

Using linearisation, regression and infinite geometric series to tackle a pharmacological problem illustrates creativity and initiative above âsomeâ yet not quite âoutstandingâ. The modelling choices are interesting but remain within standard SL techniques, placing the strand in band 2.

The exploration follows a recognisable narrative (introduction â methodology â results â conclusion) and the reader can generally see how one section leads to the next. However, transitions are sometimes abrupt, especially when the text moves from the data extraction stage to the pharmacokinetic modelling, which interrupts the logical flow. Occasional digressions (e.g., lengthy description of paracetamol packaging) further weaken the development, so the work fulfils the descriptor for âsome coherence and logical developmentâ but not the clarity required for band 3.

Critical analysis is present but basic: limitations mention reliance on secondary data and lack of participant details, yet there is limited probing of mathematical assumptions (e.g., instant absorption at 0.676 h) or error propagation. This corresponds to the âbasic analysis with limited critiqueâ descriptor, band 1.

The student ties findings back to the guiding questions, interprets concentrations in relation to therapeutic windows, and comments on real-world dosing advice, demonstrating a clear connection between reflection and mathematical understanding (band 2).

Sections are clearly sign-posted with headings, tables and graphs, and there is a discernible introduction, body and conclusion. Although some subsections feel long-winded, the overall structure is serviceable and enables the reader to find each part of the investigation without difficulty, matching the âwell organisedâ description of band 3. It is not yet âexceptionallyâ seamless, so band 4 is not justified.

A personal motivation (frequent paracetamol use) frames the investigation, and decisions such as focusing on adult over-the-counter dosage reflect the studentâs own direction. This personal approach clearly drives the work, fitting band 2. It does not yet âenhance understandingâ to the exceptional degree required for band 3.

annotations.comment: The introduction contains lengthy digressions into packaging and availability that interrupt logical flow; consider focusing on pharmacokinetics.
In Table 1, overscored numerals and mixed formatting reduce readability; standardise to simple decimal notation.
The pharmacokinetic model $$P(t)=C_{max}e^{-k_{el}(t-b)}$$ is well structured and captures absorption and elimination phases.
Definitions of \(k_{el}\) and \(k_{\alpha}\) are conflated, causing confusion; clarify which constant governs elimination.
Several entries in the ln(P) column contain typographical errors (e.g., '0/920'); verify and correct all logarithmic values.
Axes in the linearisation graph lack unit labels and regression equation annotation; include these to aid interpretation.
The title page clearly states the investigation focus and examination session, establishing context effectively.
Remove the placeholder candidate name to streamline the title page and comply with final submission requirements.
Including Figure 1 helps visualise the context of over-the-counter paracetamol, enhancing reader engagement.
Break the long introductory paragraph into shorter sections to improve readability and maintain relevance throughout.
The guiding questions are clearly articulated and directly align with the investigationâs aim.
Consider formulating specific hypotheses for each guiding question to provide measurable predictions before analysis.
The methodology section appropriately sources primary concentrationâtime data from Tanner et al. (2010), demonstrating sound research practice.
Ensure your discussion explicitly references key features of Figure 2 when describing the pharmacokinetic profile you extract.
The exponential regression equation $$C = ae^{bt}$$ is correctly presented and aligns with first-order kinetics.
Notation for constants \(a\) and \(b\) appears inconsistently later; maintain consistent symbols and definitions throughout.
When using GeoGebra for point extraction, specify the expected precision or error margin introduced by digital tracing.
Table 1 neatly organises the extracted data, facilitating clear subsequent analysis.
The table uses decorative symbols for data labels; adopt standard numeric typesetting for clarity and consistency.
Figure 6 demonstrates a highly linear ln(P) vs t plot (rââ0.998), substantiating the first-order decay assumption.
Slope calculation uses two extreme points rather than a best-fit method; apply linear regression to improve accuracy.
Y-intercept is computed by rounding intermediate values; consider extracting c directly from regression output.
The final exponential model $$P(t)=13.2e^{-0.283(t-0.676)}$$ is clearly stated with appropriate parameter labels.
The cumulative dosage section would benefit from explicitly defining the domain piecewise for each dosing interval.
Term-by-term addition of doses replicates a geometric series; streamline using sigma notation to generalise the summation.
Discuss the margin of safety between the calculated maximal concentration and toxicity threshold (>75 Âµg/mL) to deepen reflection.
Summarising interval outcomes in a single comparative chart would improve coherence and facilitate direct comparison.
In the minimal concentration model, verify that the shift parameter \(b\) corresponds to 12 hours rather than 4.676 h as used.
Clarify the geometric exponent index for the minimal series (e.g., \(r^{n-4.676}\)) to avoid misapplication of sequence rules.
Reflect on the clinical insignificance of minimal concentrations (<1 Âµg/mL) in relation to the therapeutic window (10â20 Âµg/mL).
The conclusion paragraph is overly long and dense; restructure into concise bullet points summarising key findings.
The finite series formula $$S_n=\frac{u_1(r^n-1)}{r-1}$$ is misstated for infinite summation; revise to $$S_\infty=\frac{u_1}{1-r}\,. $$
When deriving the infinite sum, the denominator should not include \(r^n\); ensure formula for $$S_\infty$$ uses only \(1-r\).
Using prescribed ibuprofen dosing to contextualise intervals demonstrates thoughtful real-world engagement with the model.
The calculation of $$S_\infty\approx19.5\,\mu g/mL$$ at 4-hour intervals aptly confirms proximity to the therapeutic maximum.
Table 3 concisely summarises minimal concentration outcomes, supporting clear interpretation of long-term dosing effects.
Limitations discussion could be deepened by addressing modelling assumptions such as instant absorption and single-compartment approximation.
